notes.anchor: the eighth day before the Kalends of November
notes.note: Reckoned inclusively backward from the Kalends (1st), this is 25 October 58 BC; the dating runs through the letter as Cicero collates Atticus's three letters.

notes.anchor: the law was promulgated by the eight tribunes of the plebs
notes.note: Eight of the ten tribunes of 58 BC drafted and posted the first bill for Cicero's recall; "promulgate" (promulgare) means to publish a bill's text and so begin the mandatory waiting period before a vote.
Promulgatio was the formal posting of a proposed law for the trinundinum (three market-days, about 24 days) so the people could consider it before voting.

notes.anchor: that bill of the old tribunes had three clauses
notes.note: The "old" or outgoing tribunes of 58 BC; a rogatio (bill) was put to the people clause by clause (caput, "head"). Cicero dissects its three heads in turn.

notes.anchor: nothing is restored except citizenship and rank
notes.note: The first clause restored Cicero's civic status (civitas) and senatorial standing (ordo) but not, he complains, his confiscated property or his razed house, which it left dangerously unaddressed.

notes.anchor: The second clause is the standard one on indemnity
notes.note: A tralaticium (boilerplate) clause carried over from earlier statutes, granting immunity to anyone who in enacting this law transgressed some other law.

notes.anchor: the protective clauses of laws to be abrogated are never observed
notes.note: A sanctio was the self-protective clause by which a law sought to bar its own repeal; to abrogate (abrogare) a law is to annul it wholly by a fresh vote of the people.
The four Roman verbs of legislation: promulgare, to publish a bill; abrogare, to repeal a law entirely; derogare, to repeal part of one; obrogare, to override one by passing a contrary law.
A plebiscite (plebis scitum) was a measure passed by the plebeian assembly; since the lex Tributa of 287 BC it bound the whole people, so Clodius's exile law had full statutory force.

notes.anchor: by the law of their own college they were not bound
notes.note: The ten tribunes formed a single collegium; the self-protective clause of Clodius's law did not penalize fellow tribunes, so the eight ran no personal risk in the wording they inserted.

A contio was an informal public meeting at which a magistrate addressed the people without a vote; Clodius used it to argue that the inserted clause itself conceded the difficulty of repealing his law.
L. Ninnius Quadratus, tribune of 58 BC and the first to move Cicero's recall; Cicero wonders how so dangerous a clause slipped past so loyal a watchman.

notes.anchor: look at the law that Visellius drafted for T. Fadius
notes.note: Visellius (a jurist and cousin of Cicero) drafted a rival recall bill for the tribune-elect T. Fadius; Cicero prefers it to the version sponsored by Sestius.

notes.anchor: on Crassus, on Pompey, on the rest
notes.note: M. Licinius Crassus and Pompey, the great men whose hesitation Atticus reckoned was holding the recall back; Crassus was lukewarm, Pompey not yet committed.
